segs.utf8:
Transcriber: Dang Ngo Hai
Reviewer: NGUYEN MAI TAN� DCVX

My son Giovanni and I, we work in
an environment where

many people dislike us,
yell at us, argue with us,

and some might even threaten us
with physical violence.
Welcome to the world of a baseball umpire.

What started out as a fun way
to spend more time with my son turned out

to be one of the most profound
learning experiences I've had dealing
with people in conflict.

And it all started when my son
Giovanni, was 11 years old.

He looked at me, he said,
dad, I want to be a baseball umpire.
We put him through the all day clinic

and after that he was set
up to umpire his first game.
And I was so proud of him.
his first job at 11 years old.

Watching him work with parents,
coaches and players.

I could see those life skills just sinking
in. Where else can you get that kind
of experience?
But the first few games,

he'd come to the backstop and he'd grip
the chain link fence and he'd say, dad,
dad,
I don't think I can do this.

I looked him in the eye and said,
you can do this, Giovanni.
Don't quit. It'll get easier next time.

After a few deep breaths, he'd go
back to the game and get through it.
At the end of the season, he said, dad,

why don't you become
a baseball umpire with me?
I said, what a great idea.
I already go to watch his games.

Not to mention the money
you'll pay for the gas.

So I went up to the umpire
in chief and said,
do you guys take adults into your program?

Yes. There's high turnover with Umpires
would take all the help we can get.
I thought high turnover.
How hard can this be?
So I signed up for my first clinic.
Okay,

you have to picture
this there I was on the field
at the clinic doing the exercises.
Me and 3511 year olds.

Have you ever heard the saying,
kids can be so mean?
Well, this is an understatement.

They were merciless. And with me,
they must have smelled weakness.

At 47 years old, I was like a cow thrown
into a tank of piranhas.
They called me loser, old man and Boomer.

But I was undeterred because I
wanted to set a good example for my son.

Our first baseball game together,
they put us in the highest,

most competitive league
there is in baseball Little League.
I arrived all dressed up in my equipment.
I was behind the plate.
Giovanni was on the basis.

I waited for the first pitch to come
in right over the plate above the knees.
Strike.

Next pitch comes
in way low to the left ball.
This was a piece of cake.
I was a natural.
Next pitch comes in right above the plate.

But was it above the knees
or below the knees?
I couldn't tell. Strike!
Audible groans from the audience.
I've been a people pleaser my entire life,

but being a baseball umpire,
I was forced to deal with something.
I wasn't used to disagreement.

But that's where the real
lessons came in. The next pitch came

in way low ball. The scorekeeper
behind me yells, hey!
UMP! He swung the bat!
This was more difficult than I expected.

He swung the bat right
in front of my eyes and I missed it.

And that's when things
really went downhill.
Next pitch comes in. Strike three.

Some guy in the stands
jumps up and yells, hey, ump!
The strike zone's at the knees.
The coach yells at me.
He goes, that's it! I'm sick of this.
What the heck are you doing?

Some guy behind me is like, hey, I'm
clean out your eyes, whatever that means.

It turns out people have strong
opinions about this strike zone.

I came to the field
with my protection, my mask,
my chest guard, my leg guards.

But I didn't have anything
to protect me emotionally.

A few games later, a high stakes
play was developing on first base.

I was on bases and Giovanni
was behind the plate.

The first baseman was
reaching for the ball

and I clearly saw the ball get
caught before the runner touched the base.
Clear cut. You're out.
The coach loses his mind.

He throws his hat in the dirt, red
faced veins bulging right up to my face.
He goes, you really messed up that call.
Said I right here. I was right here.
I had a perfect view.

He started arguing a bit
and Giovanni came over.
But I shoot him away.
Eventually I sent the coach packing.

After the game, Giovanni,
I were walking home and I said to him,
I'm sorry, man,
I can't.

I can't hack this.
It's not even worth the money.

He looked at me in the eyes and he said,
you can do this, dad.
Don't quit. It'll get easier next time.

It's shocking the moment
you realize your 12 year old
son is more clever than you are.
No, no, Giovanni, this is different.
But I just can't handle it.

The guy was right in my face,
yelling, look me in the eyes!
Lied to me just so his team could win.

Giovanni said, why
would he lie to you, dad?

He said, from where I was standing
behind the plate, I could see

the first baseman's
foot come off the base.
The coach was right. He was safe.
Well, why didn't you tell me?
I did tell you, dad, I tried.
You just weren't listening.
Giovanni was always somewhat precocious,
but I wasn't about to get schooled by him.

I said, okay, if the pitcher
wants it one way,
the batter wants it the other who's right?
He looked at me and said, they both are.
Dad.

It's shocking the moment you
realize your 12 year old son is wiser
than you are.

A few games later, a coach was irate
at me for the call I made on third base.

He was yelling at me, so I sent him
to the sidelines with a warning.

I went up to his assistant
coach and I said,

there's no excuse
for that kind of behavior.
He said, yeah, you're right.
There is no excuse for that.
If it makes a difference.
It really wasn't about you.

It was about something
bad happened to him earlier.

No, I'm not saying
that behavior is acceptable.
It's not.

But if we take these things
into consideration,
maybe what somebody's going through.

I think that's a big,
important part of being fair,

impartial and empathetic
both on and off the field.

I've also come to learn
that as a baseball umpire,

it's not just about
making the right calls.

It's about realizing
that every single person, every player,

every coach comes to the field
with their own experiences,
their own challenges and their own views.
And sometimes, just like in life,

those views are shaped by things
we can't see from where we're standing.

My son was teaching me empathy,
and if I was going to do this job,

I'd have to realize that everybody
there just loves baseball.

Of course, knowing this and putting it
into practice are two different things.

The first thing I had to learn was how
to stay calm under pressure.
Historically, when I encountered conflict,

I'd either avoid it or dig
in my heels and get defensive.

But by staying calm under pressure
hasn't just made me a better umpire.

It's made me a better father, a better
spouse, and a better business partner.
Perspective and empathy.

They're not just
valuable things on the field.

They're essential
for every conflict we encounter.

So next time you get in a disagreement,
think like an umpire.
Stay calm under pressure and ask yourself,
am I seeing the whole picture?
Can I look at this from a different angle?

And am I able to understand
where somebody else is coming from?
Maybe like me, you'll see that empathy

and perspective can be the difference
between a heated argument
and a moment of connection. Thank you.
